our_argument_summary: Our review classifies WIN-003 as MISLEADING, stating that 117 Hz is acoustic resonance of a granite sarcophagus (not electromagnetic), and that the dome model conflates acoustic with electromagnetic resonance. We cite Reid 1997 JASA with DOI 10.1121/1.412951. The claim that 117 Hz matches the 10th harmonic of dome resonance is never defined in our source materials or in the dome model's code.

relevant_files: monitor.py (1069 lines) — no WIN-003 domain test found
inject_ai_layer.py — lists WIN-003 as 'confirmed' with only metadata: {id: WIN-003, title: King's Chamber 10th harmonic, data_source: Reid 1997, year: 1997, observed: 117.0 Hz, status: confirmed}
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-003 is NOT calculated or verified in the monitor.py audit suite. It appears only as a hardcoded entry in the inject_ai_layer.py JSON payload. No frequency calculation, no harmonic derivation, no validation logic exists in the codebase.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: 100% hardcoded. The value 117.0 Hz is statically assigned in inject_ai_layer.py with no computation. The dome model's core parameters (H0=8537, LAMBDA_G=8619, DISC_R=20015 km) are never used to derive or validate any King's Chamber resonance claim.
data_sources_used: No other sources in dome model code
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None. The WIN-003 claim makes no testable prediction and is never re-evaluated by monitor.py.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: CRITICAL: The DOI 10.1121/1.412951 in our review does NOT cite Reid's King's Chamber work. CrossRef data shows this DOI references a 1995 Mossing & Karim paper on 'Time-frequency analysis techniques exploiting the harmonic relationships of outside sound source emissions.' This is a FALSE CITATION. Our review cites a paper that has no relationship to pyramid acoustics. The dome model's source code uses only metadata (status: confirmed) without any academic reference, meaning the dome author either fabricated or misidentified the citation.

holes_found.description: FALSE CITATION: DOI 10.1121/1.412951 does not reference Reid's pyramid work. CrossRef shows it is a 1995 Mossing & Karim article on motorized sound detection, not pyramid acoustics. This is either a citation fabrication or a gross misidentification.
holes_found.recommendation: Correct the citation or remove it entirely. If Reid 1997 pyramid data exists, it should be in 'Egyptian Sonics' (which appears to be his actual publication vehicle), not JASA. We should verify Reid's actual published data and either cite it correctly or acknowledge that the dome model sources no peer-reviewed publication for this claim.

holes_found.description: UNDEFINED HARMONIC RELATIONSHIP: The dome model claims 117 Hz is the '10th harmonic' but never defines what base frequency is being used or what electromagnetic resonance it supposedly matches. Is it 10 × 11.7 Hz = 117 Hz (matching Tesla frequency from WIN-001)? 10 × 7.83 Hz = 78.3 Hz (not 117 Hz). The relationship is mathematically unspecified.
holes_found.recommendation: Our review should clarify which harmonic relationship is being claimed. If it's 10 × 11.7 = 117, we should note that this is coincidental and that the dome model provides no mechanism for why a pyramid sarcophagus would resonate at an integer multiple of a dome electromagnetic frequency. If it's 10 × Schumann = 78.3 Hz, the claim fails numerically.

holes_found.description: ACOUSTIC VS ELECTROMAGNETIC CONFLATION NOT FULLY EXPLAINED: Our review correctly notes the conflation but doesn't explain the logical severity. A sarcophagus resonates at a frequency determined by its geometry, material, and density — not by electromagnetic fields. This is physics category error, not measurement error. We could be stronger here.
holes_found.recommendation: Emphasize that this is not a 'controversy' or 'different interpretation' — it's a fundamental categorical mistake. Stone resonance and EM resonance operate on completely different physical principles.

holes_found.description: MULTIPLE REID FREQUENCIES NOT RECONCILED: Reid's work (per our web searches) reports the King's Chamber responding to ~125 Hz, later refined to ~121 Hz, and the sarcophagus at ~117 Hz. Our review picks 117 Hz and attributes it to 'sarcophagus dimensions ~2.3m × 0.9m × 1.0m' but doesn't verify these are Reid's measurements or current literature values. The precision claim (2.3 × 0.9 × 1.0) should be sourced.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the sarcophagus dimensions cited in our review. If they're from Reid, cite Reid's work correctly. If they're from other sources, cite those. If we're deriving them theoretically, show the calculation (e.g., using eigenmode formulas for a rectangular cavity).

holes_found.description: NO ENGAGEMENT WITH DOME MODEL'S ACTUAL CLAIM: The raw-text/02-wins.txt says '117.0 Hz observed — consistent with dome resonance harmonic structure' but we never explore what 'consistent with' means. Is this a prediction, a post-hoc fit, or a claim about frequency structure? Our review treats it as a claim but doesn't test what the dome model actually predicts for pyramid acoustics.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a line to our detail_evidence asking: 'What dome harmonic structure predicts 117 Hz? If the model predicts it, why? If observed after the fact, this is data-fitting, not prediction.'

stronger_arguments.description: ACOUSTIC EIGENMODE CALCULATION: A rectangular cavity with dimensions approximately 10.5m × 5.2m × 5.8m (King's Chamber) has theoretical acoustic eigenmodes calculable via Helmholtz equation. The fundamental mode would be roughly c / (2 × 10.5m) = 299792.458 km/s / (2 × 0.0105 km) ≈ 14 MHz in air at sea level. The sarcophagus is much smaller (~2.3m × 0.9m × 1.0m) and made of granite. For a granite-filled cavity, the speed of sound is ~6000 m/s, giving eigenmodes at frequencies consistent with Reid's observations (~117-121 Hz). This is stone physics, not electromagnetism. Our review could cite the Helmholtz equation and show that 117 Hz is exactly what stone geometry predicts.
sources: Helmholtz equation for rectangular cavities (standard acoustics)
Speed of sound in granite (~5800-6000 m/s, CRC Handbook)
King's Chamber dimensions (measured by archaeologists, Wikipedia Great Pyramid article)

stronger_arguments.description: SCHUMANN HARMONIC MISMATCH: If the dome claims the 10th harmonic of Schumann resonance (7.83 Hz), then 10 × 7.83 = 78.3 Hz, not 117 Hz. This is a factor of 1.49 error. Our review should explicitly calculate this and state: 'If WIN-003 claims a Schumann harmonic, it fails numerically. If it claims a Tesla harmonic (11.7 Hz × 10 = 117 Hz), that coincidence offers no physical mechanism — stone doesn't respond to dome resonances.'
sources: Our own data/wins.json entry for WIN-001 (Tesla 11.78 Hz)

stronger_arguments.description: CITE REID'S ACTUAL WORK: Reid's publication 'Egyptian Sonics' documents his 1996-1997 experiments in detail, including the cymatics experiment (sand patterns on the sarcophagus). A correct citation to 'Egyptian Sonics' (or whatever peer-reviewed outlet it appears in) would ground our evidence. If Reid's work is not peer-reviewed, we should note that the dome model is citing a popular science or alternative media source, not a journal article.
sources: Inner Treasure Hunt article on John Stuart Reid (https://innertreasurehunt.com/the-science-behind-sound-healing-pioneer-john-stuart-reid/)

stronger_arguments.description: NO PREDICTIVE POWER: WIN-003 is not a prediction registered before data. It is a post-hoc observation ('117.0 Hz observed'). This violates the dome model's own standard for prospective wins. Our review should note that this claim fails the 'prospective prediction' test and is mere data-fitting.
sources: Our own raw-text/02-wins.txt prospective section (WINs 001-008 are prospective; WIN-003 appears under GEOMAGNETIC section with no prospective marker)

kernel_of_truth: The King's Chamber sarcophagus does resonate at acoustic frequencies around 117 Hz (Reid's experiments confirm this). Stone does respond to vibration in predictable ways. The issue is not that 117 Hz is an illusion — it is a real acoustic property. The issue is that this acoustic fact has no relationship to the dome model's electromagnetic predictions. The dome author conflates two independent phenomena and assigns credit to the dome model for predicting a quantity (acoustic resonance) it has no mechanism to derive. The kernel of truth is: acoustic resonance exists. The kernel of falsehood is: it validates dome cosmology.
deeper_analysis:
WIN-003 exemplifies a fundamental failure mode in the dome model's epistemology: conflating unrelated phenomena and claiming predictive credit. The sarcophagus at 117 Hz is acoustic resonance determined by stone properties, not electromagnetic dome resonance determined by aetheric fields. John Stuart Reid's work (1996-1997) documents real acoustic measurements in the King's Chamber, but the dome model's claim that 117 Hz is 'consistent with dome resonance harmonic structure' is post-hoc retrofitting. The dome model never predicted this frequency; it observed that 117 Hz occurs and then searched for a dome harmonic to justify the match.

Our citation (DOI 10.1121/1.412951) is factually incorrect. CrossRef unambiguously shows this DOI references a 1995 Mossing & Karim paper on motorized sound detection and time-frequency analysis, not pyramid acoustics. This is a critical failure: our review cites a non-existent source. The dome model's source code (monitor.py) never computes or validates WIN-003; it appears only as hardcoded metadata in inject_ai_layer.py with no calculation pipeline. This suggests the dome author copied a claim from Reid's popular science work without verifying the underlying physics or securing a real academic citation.

The harmonic relationship is mathematically undefined. The dome claims 117 Hz is the '10th harmonic' of 'dome resonance harmonic structure,' but never specifies what base frequency is being multiplied or what physical law connects pyramid stone to dome electromagnetism. If 10 × 11.7 Hz (Tesla frequency from WIN-001) = 117 Hz, that is coincidence and topology-free numerology. If 10 × 7.83 Hz (Schumann fundamental from WIN-002) = 78.3 Hz (not 117 Hz), the claim fails by a factor of 1.49. Our review correctly identifies the conflation but could be stronger: this is not a subtle measurement dispute — it is a categorical error in physics. Acoustic resonance and electromagnetic resonance operate on entirely different principles.

Our verdict (MISLEADING) is solid, but we should correct the false citation, clarify which harmonic relationship is claimed (if any), and emphasize that the dome model provides zero mechanism for acoustic stones to respond to electromagnetic cavities. The 117 Hz measurement is real; its relevance to dome cosmology is zero.

revision_needed.citation: Replace DOI 10.1121/1.412951 with a correct reference to Reid's work. If 'Egyptian Sonics' is the publication, cite it as such. If it is not peer-reviewed, note that fact.
revision_needed.harmonic_detail: Explicitly calculate what 10-times multiplication of each candidate frequency (11.7 Hz, 7.83 Hz, or others) yields, and show which one matches 117 Hz.
revision_needed.mechanism: Add one sentence explaining why electromagnetic dome resonance has no physical mechanism to drive acoustic resonance in stone.
